# NO BIG DEAL — BRAND VOICE BIBLE
*Built from a 60-question brand interview with Jo Deal. April 15, 2026.*
*Do not edit this document without re-interviewing Jo. This is the source of truth.*

---

## 1. VOICE PROFILE

**Five words that define the brand:**
Confident. Direct. Warm. Competent. Real.

**Who we're writing to:**
Two readers, equal priority:
- **The overwhelmed older homeowner** — has no one in their corner, doesn't understand the insurance process, just needs someone to tell them it's going to be okay and then actually make it okay
- **The new homeowner** — doesn't know what they need, what their policy covers, or who to call; needs clarity and plain-English answers before they can even ask the right questions

Secondary: The curious retail customer who's doing research and hasn't decided anything yet.

**Who NBD is never mistaken for:**
- A franchise or private equity rollup
- A storm chaser who disappears after the check clears
- A fear-based salesperson who bashes competitors to close
- A website written by AI and approved by nobody

---

## 2. LANGUAGE RULES

### Use these — Jo says them naturally:
- "No big deal" as a reassurance, never dismissal
- "I'll handle it" / "I'll take care of it"
- "It's just me"
- "Start to finish"
- "Insurance restoration" (not just "roofing")
- "Versed in" (signals expertise without arrogance)
- "Retail roofing" vs. "insurance restoration" — the distinction that proves expertise
- "Your project" / "your claim" — personal ownership
- "Joe Deal" — full name used naturally; the name is the brand
- "Seriously" — as emphasis at the end of a promise
- "I know claims"
- "Legitimate damage"

### Never use these:
- "Simple Process, Zero Stress" — generic template, currently on every service page
- "Real Homeowners, Real Results" — pure filler
- "We pride ourselves on..."
- "Comprehensive solutions"
- "World-class" / "industry-leading" / "state-of-the-art"
- "Insurance pays for everything" — false, and Jo hates it
- "Chuck in a truck" competitor fear-bashing
- Any line that works unchanged on a competitor's site

### Sentence structure:
Mix short punchy lines (3–7 words) with conversational mid-length sentences (15–25 words).
Write like you're explaining it in someone's driveway.

### Formality level:
**6–7 out of 10.** Professional neighbor giving real advice.
Not "Hey bro." Not "Dear Homeowner."

### Profanity:
Almost zero. Rare enough that when it appears, it means something.
No more than 1–2 instances site-wide, only when nothing else lands.

---

## 3. CORE MESSAGES

**The one-sentence brand promise:**
> "I'll make sure your project is no big deal. Seriously."

**The "I'm not like other roofers" statement:**
> "It's just me — and I've been doing this on my own for almost 7 years. I learned what works and what doesn't in the field, not in a training room. No middleman, no random crew, no project manager who's never touched a roof. You get me, start to finish."

**The core fear we solve:**
You don't know who to trust, you don't understand the insurance process, and you're worried you're going to get taken advantage of. Jo solves all three.

**The process differentiator:**
> "I go through every single step personally. If you want legitimate damage covered, you want someone versed in insurance restoration — it's vastly different from retail roofing."

**The insurance company stance (honest, not corporate):**
Some carriers are better than others. But almost all have moments where they try to undervalue claims or bank on a homeowner accepting a lowball settlement or denial. Jo's job is to make sure that doesn't happen to you.

**The objection handler for "I already have someone":**
> "That's fine — I just recommend you have someone versed in insurance restoration involved in a claim. It's vastly different from retail roofing."

---

## 4. THE 4-SECOND RULE
*(Jo's own words from the interview — the single most important brief on the site)*

Every page must make a visitor feel three things within 4 seconds of landing:

1. **Sharp as a tack** — This person knows exactly what they're talking about
2. **Enthusiastic as hell** — This person actually wants to help me
3. **A master in their field** — This person has done this hundreds of times

Every headline, hero, and opening paragraph gets tested against this before publishing.
**If it doesn't hit all three — rewrite it.**

---

## 5. THE BRAND VOICE TEST

Run these three questions on every piece of copy before it goes live:

**1. The 4-Second Rule:**
Does this make the reader feel Jo is sharp, enthusiastic, and a master — within the first two lines? If not — rewrite.

**2. The Anyone Else Test:**
Could this line appear unchanged on a competitor's site? If yes — rewrite.

**3. The Driveway Test:**
Would Jo say this while standing in someone's driveway explaining the situation?
If it requires a boardroom to say it — rewrite.

---

## 6. PAGE-BY-PAGE PRIORITIES

| Priority | Page | Key Changes |
|---|---|---|
| 🔴 1 | `docs/index.html` — Homepage | New hero headline passing the 4-second test. Kill "Simple Process, Zero Stress" and "Real Homeowners, Real Results." CALL is primary CTA, not "Free Estimate" repeated 5x. Reduce mobile scroll depth. |
| 🔴 2 | `docs/services/hail-damage-insurance-claim.html` | Make this the most powerful page on the site. Jo knows claims, adjusters, Xactimate. Should feel like talking to the one person who actually gets it. |
| 🔴 3 | `docs/pro/landing.html` — NBD Pro Marketing | Inject Jo's voice and story. Kill generic SaaS copy. Remove fabricated stats. Apply 4-second rule. |
| 🔴 4 | `docs/pro/pricing.html` — NBD Pro Pricing | Add a WHY paragraph up top. Tier names need a voice pass. Give "1/3 the competition" more room. |
| 🟡 5 | All 11 core service pages | Kill template phrases on every page. Each hero sub must be service-specific. Storm damage page gets a first-48-hours section. |
| 🟡 6 | `docs/blog/index.html` | Homeowners only. Separate or remove contractor-facing content. |
| 🟡 7 | `docs/about.html` | Add the WHY (industry owners who don't know the work). Trim triple CTA to one. |
| 🟢 8 | Area pages (24) | Needs local research per city. Flag for future session. |

---

## 7. VISUAL STANDARDS

**What's right — don't touch:**
Navy (#1e3a6e / #142a52) and Orange (#e8720c). Confirmed correct by Jo.

**Trust signals that must become visible:**
- GAF Certified installer logo
- Improvifi (0% financing) partner logo
- United Restore partnership badge
- Real Google review count (only 2 real reviews — use them prominently, not buried)

**Mobile fixes needed:**
- Phone number visible above fold without scrolling
- CALL is primary CTA — must be the first button a user sees on mobile
- Homepage is too long on mobile — cut or consolidate at least 2 sections

**NBD Pro identity:**
Dark-mode Pro pages are a known intentional split (separate brand, one small link from main site).
For now: give Pro pages Jo's voice, not his visual identity. That's enough.
